sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=0, col=56), MOUSE(row=63, col=7).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 37 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I've been wasting many actions without making progress. Let me completely reconsider the problem.
I've tried:
1. Moving S squares to all 6 positions with arrow keys
2. Pressing SPACE at various positions
3. Clicking on S squares, passages, W cells, etc. with MOUSE
None of these have completed the level. The W cells are growing with each action (by 2 pixels per action).
Let me think about this problem from a completely different angle. Maybe the goal is not to complete the level by pressing SPACE or clicking on cells, but to achieve a specific configuration of the S squares relative to the maze.
Looking at the maze structure:
- The black structure forms a maze with Y and O passages
- The S squares are 5x5 blocks that can be moved with arrow keys
- The Y and O passages form a connected path through the maze
Maybe the goal is to move the S squares so they "block" or "unblock" the Y and O passages. The S squares are in "rooms" at the bottom of the maze, adjacent to the YYYOO passage.
Wait, let me look at the maze structure more carefully. The Y and O passages form a path from the top to the bottom of the maze. The S squares are at the bottom, in rooms that are part of the black structure.
Looking at row 49: YYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
The S squares are at cols 24-28 and 34-38. The YYYOO passage is at cols 29-33. The S squares are adjacent to the passage.
But looking at row 44: YYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
The YYYOO passage is at cols 29-33, but there are no S squares in this row. The S squares are only in row 49 (or row 44 when moved up).
Actually, when the S squares are at row 44, the row would be:
Row 44: YYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
Wait, that's not right. Let me check the initial state.
The initial state had S squares at (49, 19/39). So the S squares were at cols 19-23 and 39-43. The YYYOO passage is at cols 29-33. So the S squares were not adjacent to the passage in the initial state.
When I moved the S squares RIGHT once, they moved to cols 24-28 and 34-38, which are adjacent to the passage.
When I move the S squares RIGHT again, they would move to cols 29-33 and 39-43. But the YYYOO passage is at cols 29-33, so the left S square would overlap with the passage!
Wait, but the S squares can only be at cols 14-18, 19-23, 24-28, 29-33, 34-38, 39-43, or 44-48. The passage is at cols 29-33. So if the S squares are at cols 29-33, the left S square would overlap with the passage.
But I haven't tried moving the S squares to cols 29-33. Let me check if that's possible.
Actually, looking at the maze structure, the S squares are in "rooms" at the bottom. The rooms are at cols 19-23, 24-28, 29-33, 34-38, 39-43, and 44-48. The passage is at cols 29-33. So if the S squares are at cols 29-33, the left S square would overlap with the passage.
But wait, the S squares are always two separate blocks. They can't overlap with each other or with the passage. So maybe the S squares can't be at cols 29-33 because that would overlap with the passage.
Actually, let me check the valid positions for the S squares. The S squares are 5x5 blocks. The maze has rooms at the bottom where the S squares can be. The rooms are separated by the YYYOO passage.
Looking at row 49: YYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
The S squares are at cols 24-28 and 34-38. The YYYOO passage is at cols 29-33. The rooms are at cols 19-23, 24-28, 34-38, and 39-43 (and possibly 14-18 and 44-48).
Wait, the S squares are always two blocks, and they move together. So the positions are:
- (row, col1/col2) where col1 and col2 are the left columns of the two S squares
- Initial: (49, 19/39)
- After RIGHT: (49, 24/34)
- After another RIGHT: (49, 29/39) - but this would overlap with the passage!
Actually, the S squares might not be able to move to positions that overlap with the passage. Let me check if RIGHT from (49, 24/34) is valid.
Current position is (49, 24/34). Let me try RIGHT to see if it's valid.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current position: (49, 24/34)
# Try RIGHT to see if S squares can move to (49, 29/39)
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
